pages.h1: Websites bring business. We keep it running.
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Little Fight NYC builds websites for New York small businesses, fixes urgent tech, gives free consulting, and replaces bloated subscriptions with focused software clients own.

pages.title: NYC Websites, IT Support & Custom Software | Little Fight NYC
pages.description: Website design, urgent IT support, free tech consulting, and custom software for NYC small businesses that need a clear first move.
pages.h1: Pick what's broken. We take it from there.
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Little Fight NYC starts with the website, supports the tech behind it, gives free consulting, and builds focused software the business owns when generic tools no longer fit.

pages.title: Custom Local Websites NYC | Little Fight NYC
pages.description: Custom-coded websites built for your NYC business and your block. No templates, no themes, no Squarespace skins. Ships in 14 days.
pages.h1: A website that makes the phone ring.
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Little Fight builds custom local websites for NYC small businesses. Each site is coded for the business, written for the neighborhood, and built to turn attention into calls, bookings, payments, and trust.
pages.serviceName: Custom local websites for NYC small businesses

faq.question: Do I need a new website, or just a cleanup?
faq.answer: Often a cleanup. If the platform works and customers can use it, fix the message, mobile layout, and forms first. The free consult tells you which.

faq.question: Can you connect the site to booking or payments?
faq.answer: Yes. The site should connect to what makes you money: booking, calls, forms, payments, and follow-up.

faq.question: What if you miss the 14-day window?
faq.answer: If we miss the date on our side, you do not pay. We plan the launch around you.

pages.title: Small Business IT Support NYC | Little Fight NYC
pages.description: Real local IT help for NYC small businesses. Email, domains, Wi-Fi, POS, booking, payments, devices, and locked accounts, fixed fast.
pages.h1: Fast help when the basics break.
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Little Fight gives NYC small businesses real local IT help. When email, Wi-Fi, POS, booking, payments, or devices stop working, we fix them.

faq.question: Do you help when something is broken today?
faq.answer: Yes. Call first if customers, payments, bookings, email, or access are hurting now. We stop the bleeding before we talk about any bigger cleanup.

faq.question: Do you need passwords on the first call?
faq.answer: No. Never share passwords on a call or in a form. If we need access, we use a safer handoff.

pages.title: NYC Tech Consulting for Small Business | Little Fight NYC
pages.description: A free, plain-English look at your tools, bills, website, Google presence, and workflow. Learn what earns its place and what wastes money.
pages.h1: Know what to keep, cut, fix, or build.
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Little Fight gives NYC small businesses free, plain-English tech consulting. We check your tools, website, Google presence, and workflow. You learn where money leaks and what to fix first.
pages.serviceName: Tech consulting for NYC small businesses

faq.question: How long does a consult take?
faq.answer: A first call takes 20 to 30 minutes. A full audit takes one to two weeks of background work. It ends with a written report and a talk to walk through it.

faq.question: Does an audit mean I'm getting sold something at the end?
faq.answer: No. The consult is free. It ends with a written list you can take anywhere. We charge for the work, not the talk. If the right move is 'do not hire anyone yet,' we say that.

pages.title: Custom Business Software NYC | Little Fight NYC
pages.description: Focused custom software for NYC small businesses. Replace bloated subscriptions with workflow tools you own, including the code, data, hosting, and docs.
pages.h1: Stop renting software that fights the way you work.
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Little Fight builds focused software that NYC small businesses own. It replaces scattered spreadsheets, double typing, and bloated subscriptions when off-the-shelf tools no longer fit.
pages.serviceName: Custom software for NYC small businesses

faq.question: Is custom always better than software?
faq.answer: No. Good tools stay. The right move may be keep, connect, replace, or build. It depends on the work and the monthly cost.

faq.question: What can a small business system include?
faq.answer: Lead tracking, follow-up, dashboards, forms, booking or payment handoffs, staff alerts, and simple reports.

pages.title: Free Tech Audit for NYC Small Businesses | Little Fight NYC
pages.description: Book a free Tech Audit. A real person reads your website, tools, Google presence, and monthly costs — then tells you what to fix first.
pages.h1: Show us the moving parts.
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: the Tech Audit is a free, human review of your business tech — website, tools, Google, and monthly costs. You get a straight answer, not a pitch.

faq.question: Is the Tech Audit a quote?
faq.answer: No. It is a fast first read. Little Fight NYC reviews the website, tools, search visibility, and workflow before any scope or price is set.

faq.question: Should I share passwords in the Tech Audit?
faq.answer: No. Never share passwords or private details in the Tech Audit or on a call.

pages.title: Examples: Real NYC Small Business Work & Plain Answers | Little Fight NYC
pages.description: Real examples of our NYC work, plus plain answers on websites, IT support, local SEO, and software costs.
pages.h1: Proof, maps, and plain answers.
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: this is where owners see real examples of our work, find their business type, and get plain answers to the questions we hear most. Before any pitch.

pages.title: Owner Answers: NYC Small Business Tech Questions | Little Fight NYC
pages.description: Plain answers to the questions NYC owners actually ask. Websites, Google visibility, software costs, POS, email, and urgent fixes. Short, direct, no selling.
pages.h1: Plain answers, no selling.
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: a library of plain answers to real NYC small-business tech questions. What to check, what it usually is, and when to get help.

pages.title: Website Form Not Working? | Little Fight NYC
pages.description: Form messages go missing when the email path, spam filters, plugins, or domain records break. Here is what NYC small businesses should check first.
pages.h1: Why are website form messages not reaching my small business?
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: check the form settings, email path, spam filters, and domain records first. If people say they sent a form and nothing arrived, you are losing money right now. Fix that before any redesign.

faq.question: What should I check first when website forms stop arriving?
faq.answer: Check the address the form sends to, the spam folder, the plugin settings, and the domain email records. Then do a real test from your phone.

faq.question: Is a broken form a website redesign problem?
faq.answer: Usually no. A missing form message is normally an email, spam, or settings issue. Fix that before any redesign is scoped.

pages.title: Cut Small Business Software Costs | Little Fight NYC
pages.description: A simple way for small businesses to cut monthly software waste. Drop duplicate tools, keep what works, and replace only what drags.
pages.h1: How can a small business cut monthly software costs?
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: list every tool, what it costs, and who uses it. Keep the useful tools. Connect the gaps. Cut the waste. Build only what store-bought software does badly.

faq.question: What is the safest way to cut software costs?
faq.answer: List every paid tool. Confirm who uses it. Compare overlaps. Cancel or downgrade only after you map the daily work.

faq.question: Should small businesses replace every SaaS tool with custom software?
faq.answer: No. Useful tools stay. Custom only makes sense when the workaround costs more than the tool itself.

pages.title: Business Not Showing on Google Maps NYC | Little Fight NYC
pages.description: Why a real NYC business may not show on Google Maps, and what to fix across the profile, website, reviews, and service pages.
pages.h1: Why is my NYC business not showing on Google Maps?
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Google needs a complete profile, matching contact details, real reviews, and clear service pages. It has to see proof that matches how people search nearby.

faq.question: Why would a real business not appear on Google Maps?
faq.answer: The profile may be incomplete or unverified. It may not match the website. It may be missing reviews, using the wrong category, or short on local proof.

faq.question: Does the website affect Google Maps visibility?
faq.answer: Yes. Google compares the profile with the website, service pages, contact details, and trust signals.

pages.title: Save Money on Salon Booking Software NYC | Little Fight NYC
pages.description: How NYC salons can cut booking software costs while keeping appointments, payments, reminders, and follow-up simple.
pages.h1: How can an NYC hair salon save money on booking software?
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: keep the booking tool if staff and clients really use it. Save by cutting duplicate tools, cleaning the service menu, and connecting follow-up. Replace spreadsheets only where they slow you down.

faq.question: Should a salon cancel its booking platform to save money?
faq.answer: Not unless staff and clients can still book, pay, reschedule, and get reminders without confusion.

faq.question: Where do salon software costs usually hide?
faq.answer: In duplicate marketing tools, unused add-ons, hand-kept spreadsheets, payment fees, and messy service menus.

pages.title: Local Pharmacy Website Support NYC | Little Fight NYC
pages.description: How a local pharmacy website can make hours, services, refills, insurance notes, and the phone number easier to find.
pages.h1: How can a local pharmacy website better support its community?
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: make hours, services, refills, insurance notes, and the phone number easy to find. A pharmacy site should clear up confusion before the customer calls or walks in.

faq.question: What matters most on a local pharmacy website?
faq.answer: Hours, the phone number, refills, services, insurance notes, and the location. All easy to find.

faq.question: Should a pharmacy website push customers into a portal first?
faq.answer: Only when the portal is the clearest path. Many visitors need quick trust, a phone number, or refill steps first.

pages.title: Custom Business Systems vs SaaS | Little Fight NYC
pages.description: When a right-sized custom system beats another SaaS subscription for small teams stuck with spreadsheets and hand-done follow-up.
pages.h1: When does a custom business system beat another subscription?
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: custom may make sense when you pay for a big platform but still lean on spreadsheets, double typing, hand-done follow-up, or reports nobody trusts.

faq.question: When does custom software make sense for a small business?
faq.answer: When you already pay for tools but still lose time to double typing, hand-done follow-up, spreadsheet gaps, or reports nobody trusts.

faq.question: What should happen before building a custom system?
faq.answer: Map the work. Add up the real cost of the workaround. Keep the useful tools. Then define the smallest system that removes the drag.

pages.title: Best Web Designer NYC? What Reddit Actually Says
pages.description: Reddit says check portfolios, vet the person, get scope in writing. Here is what that advice misses for NYC small businesses — and how to actually choose.
pages.h1: Best Web Designer NYC? What Reddit Actually Says
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Reddit's advice is to check real portfolios, ask who actually does the work, and walk away from anyone who talks in jargon. That holds up. What it misses is fit: the right designer for a Midtown law firm is the wrong one for a Bushwick coffee shop.

faq.question: How do I check if a web designer is legit?
faq.answer: Ask for live sites they built, contact one past client, and confirm the domain and hosting will be in your name. Anyone solid welcomes all three requests.

faq.question: Should I hire a freelancer or an agency?
faq.answer: Neither label guarantees anything. Judge the actual person doing the work, their real portfolio, and whether the scope and timeline are in writing.

faq.question: What should I bring to a first conversation?
faq.answer: One sentence on what the site must do, a few sites you like, and your current logins situation. That is enough for an honest scoping talk.

pages.title: Best Web Design Agency NYC — a Reddit Roundup
pages.description: Reddit says most small businesses skip the big agency — mostly right. When an NYC agency is worth it, when a small studio wins, and the questions to ask.
pages.h1: Best Web Design Agency NYC — a Reddit Roundup
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Reddit says most small businesses do not need a big agency, and that is mostly right. Agencies earn their keep on large scopes. For a storefront or small team, what matters is who personally does your work and what they promise in writing.

faq.question: Do agencies have minimum budgets?
faq.answer: Many do, formally or not. If your project is under their usual scope, you risk becoming the account nobody prioritizes. Ask directly before signing anything.

faq.question: How do I know who will actually build my site?
faq.answer: Ask for names and roles of everyone touching the project, and whether any work is subcontracted. A trustworthy shop answers plainly.

faq.question: Is a small studio riskier than a big agency?
faq.answer: Not inherently. The risk in both cases is the same: unclear scope, no written timeline, and accounts not in your name. Fix those and size matters much less.

pages.title: Small Business IT Support NYC: Reddit Recommendations, Vetted
pages.description: Reddit's IT support advice: local, responsive, no long contracts. We vet it for NYC storefronts, where response time and on-site help decide everything.
pages.h1: Small Business IT Support NYC: Reddit Recommendations, Vetted
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Reddit's standard advice is solid. Find responsive local help, avoid long contracts, keep your own passwords. The part it undersells: in NYC, response time is everything. A remote-only provider cannot fix the dead router in your basement.

faq.question: Do I need managed IT support for a small shop?
faq.answer: Usually not the enterprise version. Most NYC storefronts need reliable on-call help, documented accounts, and fast on-site response — not a full managed contract.

faq.question: What response time should I expect from IT support?
faq.answer: Get it in writing. Ours is a two-hour callback between 9am and 9pm ET and on-site within twenty-four hours. Vague promises like fast response mean nothing.

faq.question: What should I do before switching IT providers?
faq.answer: Collect every password, account, and renewal into a list the business owns. Never switch while your only copy of the keys lives with the old provider.

pages.title: How to Find a Good IT Guy — What Reddit Gets Right (and Wrong)
pages.description: Reddit is right about referrals and test jobs, wrong to stop there. The real tests: accounts in your name, written response times, plain-English answers.
pages.h1: How to Find a Good IT Guy — What Reddit Gets Right (and Wrong)
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Reddit is right that referrals and a small test job beat any directory. It is wrong to stop there. The real test of an IT person is whether your accounts stay in your name and whether they explain things you can repeat back.

faq.question: Is a solo IT person riskier than an IT company?
faq.answer: Only if they are a single point of failure. Documentation, accounts in your name, and written response times matter more than headcount.

faq.question: What if my old IT guy controls my accounts?
faq.answer: Start recovery now, while things are calm. Domains, email, and hosting can usually be reclaimed with proof of ownership, but it takes longer under pressure.

faq.question: How should I test a new IT provider?
faq.answer: Give them one small paid job and watch three things: how fast they respond, whether the fix holds, and whether you understood their explanation.

pages.title: Squarespace vs Hiring a Designer: the Reddit Debate, Settled Honestly
pages.description: Reddit's Squarespace-vs-designer debate, settled honestly: when DIY is genuinely enough, when hiring pays, and why owner time is the real budget in NYC.
pages.h1: Squarespace vs Hiring a Designer: the Reddit Debate, Settled Honestly
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Reddit is right that Squarespace is enough for many simple businesses — and right that most owners never finish the DIY site they start. The honest question is not which tool wins. It is whether your evenings are the budget.

faq.question: Is Squarespace good enough for a small business website?
faq.answer: Often yes — if your needs are simple and you will actually finish and maintain it. The tool is rarely the bottleneck; owner time is.

faq.question: When is hiring a designer worth it?
faq.answer: When the site must generate revenue and you would rather run your business than build web pages. A stalled DIY site has a real cost in missed customers.

faq.question: Can a designer build on Squarespace so I can edit it later?
faq.answer: Yes, and it is a fair middle path: professional structure and local search setup, with day-to-day edits staying easy and in your hands.

pages.title: Wix vs Custom Website — Reddit's Take vs Ours
pages.description: Reddit is right: the Wix-hurts-SEO claim is outdated, but the lock-in is real. When Wix is enough for an NYC business and when a custom site earns its cost.
pages.h1: Wix vs Custom Website — Reddit's Take vs Ours
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Reddit is right that the old Wix-is-bad-for-Google claim is outdated, and right that you cannot take a Wix site with you when you leave. Wix is fine as a starting point. Custom earns its cost when the website is a revenue channel.

faq.question: Is Wix bad for SEO?
faq.answer: Not anymore in the way old threads claim. The platform basics are fine. Rankings depend far more on your content, local proof, and Google profile than the builder.

faq.question: Can I move my Wix site somewhere else later?
faq.answer: Not really — content can be copied out by hand, but the site itself does not export. Leaving Wix means rebuilding, so factor that into the decision.

faq.question: When is a custom website worth it for a small business?
faq.answer: When the site drives real revenue and a template is measurably costing you — slow pages, weak local visibility, or integrations that fight you.

pages.title: Is Local SEO Worth It? Reddit's Verdict for NYC
pages.description: Reddit's verdict holds up: SEO agencies often waste money, and local SEO is real. What NYC owners can do themselves and where paid help actually earns it.
pages.h1: Is Local SEO Worth It? Reddit's Verdict for NYC
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Reddit's verdict is that SEO agencies are often a waste but local SEO itself is real — and that is basically correct. For NYC businesses, showing up in map results and neighborhood searches is worth serious effort. Much of it you can do yourself.

faq.question: Can I do local SEO myself?
faq.answer: Most of it, yes: complete your Google Business Profile, gather steady real reviews, use real photos, and describe your services and neighborhood plainly on your site.

faq.question: How long does local SEO take to show results?
faq.answer: Profile fixes can move map visibility within weeks; content and review momentum builds over months. Anyone promising instant rankings is selling something.

faq.question: How do I know if an SEO provider is legit?
faq.answer: They tie work to outcomes you can see: calls, direction requests, ranking for named searches. And they can explain every task in plain English.

pages.title: Google Business Profile Tips Reddit Swears By — Checked by a Pro
pages.description: Reddit's favorite Google Business Profile tips, checked by a pro: what actually moves NYC map rankings, what barely matters, and what risks suspension.
pages.h1: Google Business Profile Tips Reddit Swears By — Checked by a Pro
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Reddit's favorite tips are correct. Complete every field, use real photos, keep reviews steady, and pick the right category. The one it undersells: your primary category and review replies matter more than posting frequency. And keyword-stuffing your business name can get you suspended.

faq.question: Do Google Business Profile posts help ranking?
faq.answer: Barely, in our experience. They are worth doing for customers who read them, but category, reviews, and profile completeness matter far more.

faq.question: Is adding keywords to my business name a good idea?
faq.answer: No. It violates Google's guidelines and is a common suspension trigger. Use your real business name and put keywords in your description and services instead.

faq.question: How many reviews do I need to compete in NYC?
faq.answer: There is no magic number — steady pace beats totals. A profile gaining a few genuine reviews every month with replies outperforms a stale pile.

pages.title: Web Developer Ghosted You? Reddit's Advice, Improved
pages.description: Reddit says document and dispute. Better: secure your domain, hosting, and email first, then recover the project. A calm triage plan for stranded sites.
pages.h1: Web Developer Ghosted You? Reddit's Advice, Improved
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Reddit says document everything, dispute the payment, and warn others. Fair — but the first move is different: secure what you own. Get control of your domain, hosting, and email today, before you spend one minute on blame.

faq.question: My developer owns my domain. Can I get it back?
faq.answer: Usually yes, with patience — registrars have transfer and dispute processes, and proof like business registration and payment records helps. Start before it becomes urgent.

faq.question: Should I dispute the payment right away?
faq.answer: Not before securing access. If the developer still controls your hosting or domain, a dispute can escalate into your site going dark. Secure first.

faq.question: How do I stop this happening next time?
faq.answer: Milestone payments, accounts registered in your name from day one, and your own copies of files and credentials. Our Journal guide covers the early warning signs.

pages.title: Best POS for a Small Business: What Reddit Recommends
pages.description: Reddit recommends Square for simplicity and Toast for restaurants — fair. What the threads miss: the right POS is the one that connects to everything else.
pages.h1: Best POS for a Small Business: What Reddit Recommends
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Reddit's shorthand is a fine starting point. Square for simplicity, Toast for restaurants, and read the processing terms before signing anything. What the threads miss: the right POS depends on what it must connect to, not the logo on the terminal.

faq.question: Is Square good enough for a small business?
faq.answer: For many, yes — simple setup, predictable fees, solid basics. Its limits show in complex restaurant service and deep inventory needs, where specialized systems earn their cost.

faq.question: What POS contract terms should I watch for?
faq.answer: Early-termination fees, leased hardware you never own, and processing rates that can rise after an introductory period. Get every number in writing before signing.

faq.question: Should my POS connect to my website?
faq.answer: If you sell online or take orders, yes — one inventory, one sales record. Running the website and register as separate worlds creates daily manual work.

pages.title: Square vs Toast — the Reddit Threads, Summarized
pages.description: Reddit's Square vs Toast verdict, summarized: counters lean Square, full service leans Toast. Plus the NYC restaurant realities the threads leave out.
pages.h1: Square vs Toast — the Reddit Threads, Summarized
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Reddit's split decision is consistent — Square for counters, cafes, and simple service; Toast for full-service restaurants that live and die by kitchen flow. Both verdicts hold up. The contract terms and your service style decide it, not the brand.

faq.question: Is Toast overkill for a small cafe?
faq.answer: Often, yes. Counter-service spots rarely use the kitchen and table features that justify Toast's weight. Square-style simplicity usually fits better.

faq.question: Can Square handle a full-service restaurant?
faq.answer: Up to a point. It has restaurant features, but busy full-service spots with coursing and kitchen timing tend to outgrow it — which is where Toast earns its case.

faq.question: What should NYC restaurants check before committing?
faq.answer: Total fee math at your real volume, contract exit terms, offline mode behavior, and whether the hardware suits your building. Our Journal comparison covers each.

pages.title: GlossGenius vs Square Appointments: the Reddit Roundup
pages.description: Reddit's take: solo stylists lean GlossGenius, staffed salons lean Square Appointments. The roundup, checked against real NYC salon setups and no-shows.
pages.h1: GlossGenius vs Square Appointments: the Reddit Roundup
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Reddit's pattern is clear — solo stylists and chair renters lean GlossGenius for the polish and flat pricing; salons with staff and retail lean Square Appointments for the free tier and ecosystem. Both readings hold up in practice.

faq.question: Is GlossGenius worth it for a solo stylist?
faq.answer: Often yes — polished client-facing booking and predictable flat pricing suit independents. Do the fee math at your real volume before deciding.

faq.question: Is Square Appointments really free?
faq.answer: The solo tier is, with processing fees per payment. Costs appear as you add staff and features — reasonable, but read the tier list before building your book on it.

faq.question: What matters more than which booking app I pick?
faq.answer: Deposits, reminder settings, and a booking link connected to your Google profile. Misconfigured settings cost NYC stylists more than platform choice does.

pages.title: Shopify vs Squarespace — Reddit's Answers for NYC Retail
pages.description: Reddit says Shopify when selling is the business, Squarespace when it is presence. For NYC retail the tiebreaker is inventory sync between floor and web.
pages.h1: Shopify vs Squarespace — Reddit's Answers for NYC Retail
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Reddit's rule of thumb is right. Shopify when selling is the business, Squarespace when the site is mostly presence with some selling. For NYC retail the deciding question is inventory: if the shop floor and the website must share stock, Shopify pulls ahead.

faq.question: Is Shopify too much for a small NYC boutique?
faq.answer: Not if the floor and website share inventory — that sync is Shopify's strength. If online is a side channel, Squarespace's simplicity may serve you better.

faq.question: Why do Shopify costs keep growing?
faq.answer: Apps. The base platform is predictable, but features often live in paid add-ons. Audit your app list quarterly and cut what the business stopped using.

faq.question: Can Squarespace handle in-store pickup?
faq.answer: At a basic level, yes. If pickup, local delivery, and live stock visibility are core to your shop, that is the point where Shopify starts earning its complexity.

pages.title: Do I Even Need a Website? Reddit vs Reality in 2026
pages.description: Reddit is split on whether small businesses need websites. In 2026, search engines and AI assistants answer customers by reading them. The honest call.
pages.h1: Do I Even Need a Website? Reddit vs Reality in 2026
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Reddit is split — half say a Google profile and Instagram are enough, half say own your own ground. In 2026 the tiebreaker is that search engines and AI assistants answer customers by reading websites. If you are not there, someone else is the answer.

faq.question: Is a Google Business Profile enough without a website?
faq.answer: It is the minimum, and for some full-book businesses it suffices. But profiles backed by real websites tend to rank better and give AI search something to cite.

faq.question: Do people still visit small business websites?
faq.answer: Less as destinations, more as sources — search engines and AI assistants read them to answer customers. The site works even when nobody browses it.

faq.question: What is the smallest useful website?
faq.answer: A fast page stating what you do, where, your hours, prices context, photos, and a way to call or book. Five sections done well beats twenty done badly.

pages.title: Airtable vs Notion for Small Business: Reddit Roundup
pages.description: Reddit's rule is right: Notion for docs, Airtable for records. The harder small-business question: which tool will your least techy employee open?
pages.h1: Airtable vs Notion for Small Business: Reddit Roundup
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Reddit's rule is correct. Notion for documents and wikis, Airtable for structured records. Most fights start when someone forces one tool to do the other's job. For a small business the harder question is simpler: which one will your least techy person actually open?

faq.question: Can I run my whole business in Notion?
faq.answer: You can, but structured records like orders, clients, and inventory get fragile in it. Most teams do better with Notion for docs and something table-shaped for data.

faq.question: Is Airtable worth paying for over a spreadsheet?
faq.answer: When multiple people update the same records and you need views, filters, and forms — yes. For one person's simple list, a spreadsheet is honestly fine.

faq.question: Why do our workspaces always turn into junk drawers?
faq.answer: No owner and no pruning. Someone must be in charge of structure, and anything nobody updated in a quarter should be archived without ceremony.

pages.title: Where NYC Owners Actually Get Tech Help (Reddit Included)
pages.description: Where NYC small business owners really find tech help: referrals, Reddit, neighborhood groups, free city programs. And how to vet whoever you choose.
pages.h1: Where NYC Owners Actually Get Tech Help (Reddit Included)
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: NYC owners get tech help from referrals, neighborhood groups, Reddit threads, city small-business programs, and whoever built the last thing that worked. Referrals are the best of these — and even they only work if you know what to ask for.

faq.question: Are the city's free small-business resources worth using?
faq.answer: Yes — NYC Small Business Services runs free courses and advising that are genuinely useful for planning. They are not built for urgent technical fixes.

faq.question: How do I vet a tech provider a friend recommended?
faq.answer: Ask what they will do in plain English, what response times they put in writing, and whether every account ends up owned by your business. Then test with one small job.

faq.question: Is asking on Reddit or neighborhood groups a bad idea?
faq.answer: It is a fine way to gather names, not a way to vet them. Treat every group-chat recommendation as a lead that still needs the same three questions.

pages.title: Google Business Profile Suspended? What to Do (NYC Guide)
pages.description: Suspended Google Business Profile? Do not create a new one. Find the trigger, fix it fully, and file one complete reinstatement request. NYC recovery guide.
pages.h1: Google Business Profile Suspended? What to Do (NYC Guide)
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: do not panic-edit and do not create a new profile. Figure out which rule you tripped, fix it fully, then file one complete reinstatement request with proof your business is real. Most suspensions are recoverable — repeat appeals without fixes are not.

faq.question: How long does Google Business Profile reinstatement take?
faq.answer: Typically several days to a few weeks. One complete request with strong proof moves fastest; repeated thin appeals can slow everything down.

faq.question: Will I lose my reviews after a suspension?
faq.answer: Usually no — reviews and photos normally return when the profile is reinstated. That is one more reason not to start a new profile from scratch.

faq.question: Can I just create a new profile instead of appealing?
faq.answer: No. Duplicate profiles violate Google's rules and can get both listings removed, turning a recoverable problem into a much longer one.

pages.title: Website Down? An Emergency Checklist for NYC Businesses
pages.description: Website down? Check domain expiry, hosting billing, and SSL first — most outages are lapsed renewals, not hacks. An emergency checklist for NYC businesses.
pages.h1: Website Down? An Emergency Checklist for NYC Businesses
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: first confirm it is down for everyone, not just you. Then check the three usual suspects in order before touching anything else: domain expiry, hosting billing, SSL certificate. Most outages are a lapsed renewal, not a hack.

faq.question: How do I know if my website is down for everyone or just me?
faq.answer: Load it on your phone with wifi off, or ask someone outside your building to try. If it loads for them, the issue is your network, not the website.

faq.question: My domain expired. Is the website gone forever?
faq.answer: Almost never. Recently expired domains can usually be renewed and restored within hours. Act fast though — long-lapsed domains can be resold.

faq.question: Was my site hacked?
faq.answer: Probably not — billing lapses and expired certificates cause far more outages than attacks. But if you see defaced pages or strange redirects, treat it as a hack and get help.

pages.title: POS Down During Service? A Restaurant Triage Guide
pages.description: POS down mid-service? Sixty-second triage: internet, provider, or hardware. Then offline mode or paper to keep serving. An NYC restaurant recovery guide.
pages.h1: POS Down During Service? A Restaurant Triage Guide
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: figure out in sixty seconds whether it is your internet, your POS provider, or one dead device — the fixes are completely different. Then switch to offline mode or paper, keep serving, and do the real diagnosis after the rush, not during it.

faq.question: Can I take card payments when the internet is down?
faq.answer: Usually yes, through your POS's offline mode — payments queue and sync later, with some risk on declines. Enable and test it before the night you need it.

faq.question: Should I switch POS providers after an outage?
faq.answer: Not in anger. Every provider has outages. Switch for repeated failures your setup cannot absorb, and only after checking whether the real weakness was your internet.

faq.question: What backup gear should a small restaurant keep?
faq.answer: A charged hotspot or second internet source, a spare card reader and receipt printer, a battery backup on the router, and a printed what-to-do sheet for staff.

pages.title: Your Business Email Is Going to Spam — Here's Why
pages.description: Business email landing in spam? Your domain is likely missing SPF, DKIM, and DMARC — the ID papers inbox providers now require. Plain-English fix guide.
pages.h1: Your Business Email Is Going to Spam — Here's Why
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: your domain is probably missing its ID papers — three DNS records called SPF, DKIM, and DMARC that prove your email is really from you. Since inbox providers tightened rules in 2024, mail without them lands in spam even when it is perfectly legitimate.

faq.question: What are SPF, DKIM, and DMARC in plain English?
faq.answer: Three small settings on your domain that prove your email really comes from you. Inbox providers treat mail without them as suspicious, no matter how legitimate it is.

faq.question: Why did my email suddenly start going to spam in 2024?
faq.answer: Google and Yahoo tightened requirements for senders — authentication records, low complaint rates, easy unsubscribe. Mail that misses the bar now gets filtered much harder.

faq.question: Can I send my newsletter from my regular inbox?
faq.answer: You should not. Bulk mail from a normal inbox hurts your domain's reputation. Use a proper email service and keep your day-to-day address for conversations.

pages.title: Google Reviews Not Showing Up? The Honest Reasons
pages.description: Google reviews not showing up? Most were filtered, not lost — bursts, new accounts, and incentives trip the filter. The honest reasons and the real fixes.
pages.h1: Google Reviews Not Showing Up? The Honest Reasons
pages.shortAnswer: Short answer: Google's filter removes or hides reviews it finds suspicious. Bursts from one location, brand-new accounts, anything that smells incentivized. And it catches honest ones in the net. Most missing reviews were filtered, not lost, and prevention beats appeal.

faq.question: Why did a real five-star review disappear?
faq.answer: Most likely Google's filter — new reviewer account, same-network posting, or a burst pattern. The reviewer reposting later from their own connection often works.

faq.question: Can filtered reviews be restored?
faq.answer: Rarely. Support can look into clearly missing legitimate reviews, but the filter's decisions mostly stand. Prevention is the real fix: steady, unprompted-looking asks.

faq.question: Are QR codes asking for reviews against the rules?
faq.answer: QR codes are fine. The trouble starts when many customers post from the same location and network in a burst, or when only happy customers get the code.

glossaryTerms.definition: The connected set of pages, tools, reminders, and records that moves work from first hello to finished job.
glossaryTerms.plain: The way the work actually moves, not just the software you bought.

faq.question: Isn't a business system just more software I have to learn?
faq.answer: No. Usually it's connecting the tools you already pay for so they work together, plus cleaning up a few messy handoffs. The goal is fewer things to check, not more apps to log into.

faq.question: I'm a two-person shop. Am I too small for this?
faq.answer: Small shops benefit the most, because there's no big team to catch what falls through the cracks. When it's just you, a simple system is the difference between remembering everything and losing jobs you never knew you had.

glossaryTerms.definition: One place where leads, customers, notes, follow-up, and sales history live.
glossaryTerms.plain: The list of people who asked for help, bought something, or need a reply.

faq.question: Do I need an expensive CRM like the big companies use?
faq.answer: Almost never. Most small businesses are better off with something simple and cheap, set up to match how they actually work. A fancy CRM you don't use is worse than a plain one you do.

faq.question: I already have my customers in a spreadsheet. Isn't that enough?
faq.answer: A spreadsheet is a fine start, but it won't remind you to follow up or catch a lead the moment it comes in. A CRM adds the nudges and the shared access a growing shop needs.

glossaryTerms.definition: The free Google listing that controls how a local business appears in Search and Maps, including hours, phone, reviews, services, photos, and location.
glossaryTerms.plain: The Google card people see before they ever reach your website.

faq.question: Is this the same as having a website?
faq.answer: No, and you need both. The website is your home; the Google profile is the sign on the busy street that points people to it. Many customers check the profile first and only visit the site if the card earns their trust.

faq.question: How do I get more reviews without being pushy?
faq.answer: The simplest way is to ask happy customers at the right moment, right after a good job, usually with a quick text or a link. A small, steady habit of asking beats any trick, and it keeps the card looking active.

glossaryTerms.definition: The work that helps nearby customers find, trust, and pick your business on Google and Maps.
glossaryTerms.plain: Showing up when someone nearby is already looking.

faq.question: How long until local search actually helps me?
faq.answer: Quick wins like fixing your profile can help within weeks. Reviews and good pages build over a few months. It is a habit, not a one-time switch.

faq.question: Do I need to pay Google for ads to show up nearby?
faq.answer: No. The regular map and search results are earned, not bought. Ads can add reach on top. A well-kept profile and honest pages do most of the work for free.

glossaryTerms.definition: All the tools a business uses to run the day. Website, booking, POS, payments, email, spreadsheets, and reports.
glossaryTerms.plain: All the apps you pay for, plus the work people still do around them.

faq.question: Does fixing my stack mean ripping everything out and starting over?
faq.answer: Usually not. Most of the win comes from cutting a couple of unused tools and connecting the good ones, not a rip-and-replace. We keep what works and only replace what's genuinely dragging you down.

faq.question: How do I know if I'm paying for tools I don't use?
faq.answer: A quick pass through your card statement for recurring charges almost always turns up a surprise or two. If you can't remember the last time you opened it, that's usually your answer.

glossaryTerms.definition: A rule that moves routine work forward on its own. It can send a lead to the right place, make a follow-up task, or update a dashboard.
glossaryTerms.plain: The boring repeatable step happens without someone remembering to do it.

faq.question: Will automation make my business feel cold or robotic to customers?
faq.answer: Done right, it does the opposite. Customers get faster replies and never get forgotten. That feels more caring, not less. You automate the reminder. The relationship stays human.

faq.question: Is this only for big companies with tech teams?
faq.answer: No. The best automations for a small shop are small and cheap. One confirmation text. One follow-up reminder. You do not need a tech team. Just the right rule, set up once.
